title: The Eye That Must Become Sunlike — Ficino, Plotinus, and the Training of Beauty
author: Plotinus / Marsilio Ficino

body.text: Beauty is adiscipline by which the seer is remade. The soul must become proportionate to what it seeks. This is the Neoplatonic root of Ficino'saesthetic magic: the beautiful image trains the soul toward ahigher seeing.

Beauty addresses itself chiefly to sight; but there is abeauty for the hearing too, as in certain combinations of words and in all kinds of music, for melodies and cadences are beautiful; and minds that lift themselves above the realm of sense to ahigher order are aware of beauty in the conduct of life, in actions, in character, in the pursuits of the intellect; and there is the beauty of the virtues. What loftier beauty there may be, yet, our argument will bring to light.

Ordinary beauty — sight and music — is where the ascent starts. The beautiful also lives in action and intellect, in character and in the virtues that shape alife. Ficino inherits this as apractical law. Music trains the ear beyond sound. Proportion trains the eye beyond form. The soul detects one radiance through many surfaces.

Withdraw into yourself and look. And if you do not find yourself beautiful yet, act as does the creator of astatue that is to be made beautiful: he cuts away here, he smoothes there, he makes this line lighter, this other purer, until alovely face has grown upon his work. So do you also: cut away all that is excessive, straighten all that is crooked, bring light to all that is overcast, labour to make all one glow of beauty and never cease chiselling your statue, until there shall shine out on you from it the godlike splendour of virtue, until you shall see the perfect goodness surely established in the stainless shrine.

The self is marble containing adivine form. The work is removal — cutting away what is excessive until the hidden shape appears. Ficino'smagic operates by the same principle. The rite polishes. It clears away dullness and fragmentation. It educates spiritus until the soul can receive ahigher likeness.

body.caption: The soul as statue being chiselled toward its divine form.

body.text: Never did eye see the sun unless it had first become sunlike, and never can the soul have vision of the First Beauty unless itself be beautiful. Therefore, first let each become godlike and each beautiful who cares to see God and Beauty. So, mounting, the Soul will come first to the Intellectual-Principle and survey all the beautiful Ideas in the Supreme and will avow that this is Beauty, that the Ideas are Beauty.

The eye must become sunlike. This is the central law. Modern perception assumes seeing is passive — the world outside, information entering. Plotinus says the soul sees according to what it has become. Likeness is the condition of perception. Surround yourself with nobler images and purer sounds. Let what you study be ordered toward what the soul needs to become, because what you can perceive depends on the soul you have cultivated.

body.caption: The eye becoming sunlike — the perceiver transformed into likeness with what it beholds.

body.text: The visible world is derivative. Its beauty is real because it participates in ahigher beauty. But because it is derivative, it must be followed beyond itself. The ascetic practice: collect beautiful images, then ask what in the soul responds to them. Ask what must be carved away for the seeing eye to become sunlike. The work is to become capable of seeing why the world was luminous already.
